critic_raw:
This PoC makes several implicit claims that are not tested and that could individually invalidate its conclusions.

1. PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE UNDER THE GUISE OF STABILITY. The design explicitly states that 'all transformers (audio MFCC extraction parameters, thermal image normalization statistics, video frame standardization) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' This is data leakage. Fitting normalization on the full 12 months means the test set's mean and variance are encoded in the training-time scaler. For time-series sensor data, where statistics drift — particularly thermal and audio in a manufacturing environment subject to seasonal temperature variation and mechanical wear — this inflates the apparent stationarity of features and systematically understates generalization error. The justification ('guarantees more stable estimates') is a rationalization of leakage, not a methodological defense.

2. SINGLE-FACILITY TEMPORAL CONFOUND MASKED BY 'MACHINE-LEVEL STRATIFICATION.' The design uses stratified random sampling with 'machine-level stratification' across months, but the critical dependency structure in sensor data is temporal: failures cluster, maintenance suppresses failure rates immediately post-repair, and sensor drift is autocorrelated within machines. Random splitting at the observation level breaks these temporal dependencies, allowing the model to see pre- and post-failure sensor states from the same machine in both train and test. The validation set described as 'months 9-10' in Section 6 contradicts the random split described in Section 2 — this inconsistency suggests the split strategy is underspecified and the temporal holdout is not actually enforced.

3. BASELINE ASYMMETRY IN MODALITY ACCESS. The CNN receives audio spectrograms, thermal sequences, and optical flow simultaneously. The Random Forest baseline receives 'the identical feature set' — which in practice means hand-engineered aggregates (mean, max, std across modalities), not raw sensor streams. This is not equivalent: the CNN can learn cross-modal temporal patterns that the feature engineering step has already compressed away for the baseline. Any advantage attributed to 'CNN architecture and multi-modal integration' may simply be an advantage of richer temporal representations.

4. LABEL CONSTRUCTION CIRCULARITY IN FAILURE DEFINITION. Failures are labeled by cross-referencing sensor anomalies with maintenance events within a 48-hour window. This means that machines that failed silently (no documented repair) are labeled as normal operation, and machines that received preventive maintenance triggered by sensor readings (not actual failure) may be mislabeled as failures. The exclusion of 'ambiguous' events is not operationally defined — the threshold for what counts as ambiguous is never specified, opening the door for post-hoc label cleaning that could systematically remove hard negatives.

5. MACRO-F1 AS PRIMARY METRIC PRESUPPOSES CLASS BALANCE THAT IS NOT GUARANTEED. The design applies 'equal weighting to both classes despite the imbalance.' With a heavily skewed failure rate (typical in manufacturing: ~5-15% positive), macro-F1 will penalize a model that is operationally excellent but cautious about declaring failures. More critically, optimizing on validation F1 with early stopping means the threshold is implicitly set during training. The test F1 reported will depend on the threshold selected on validation data — which was tuned to the validation class distribution. If the test period has a different failure rate (plausible given seasonal effects), the threshold is miscalibrated and the reported F1 will not reflect operational performance.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fitted on entire dataset before splitting constitutes data leakage; fitting MFCC parameters, thermal normalization statistics, and video standardization on full data encodes test-period statistics into the scaler
Stratified random split breaks temporal dependency structure in sensor data; pre- and post-failure states from the same machine can appear in both train and test sets
Inconsistency between random split strategy (Section 2) and the temporal validation window described as months 9-10 (Section 6) — split strategy is underspecified
Baseline Random Forest receives only hand-engineered aggregates while CNN receives raw multi-modal streams; performance gap may reflect representation richness rather than architecture
Label construction via sensor-anomaly-to-maintenance-log cross-reference introduces systematic mislabeling: silent failures labeled negative, sensor-triggered preventive maintenance labeled positive
Macro-F1 as primary metric with threshold tuned on validation data is miscalibrated if failure rate shifts between validation and test periods
Preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset before split leaks test-period statistics into training normalization
Single-facility data confounds generalization claim; CNN may learn facility-specific machine fingerprints
Label construction from maintenance logs creates survivorship bias — positives are only failures that slipped through existing monitoring
Baseline receives same hand-crafted features as CNN, conflating feature engineering choice with model capacity
Globally computed rolling features straddle temporal split boundaries, introducing lookahead bias
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting causes target-aware data leakage
Stratified random split in time-series industrial data breaks temporal causality and induces future leakage
CNN and Random Forest receive different feature representations (raw vs. aggregated), confounding architecture with feature quality
Macro-averaged F1 is miscalibrated for asymmetric failure detection costs stated in the objective
Machine-level stratification under random splitting causes within-machine temporal leakage
